notes: Pick the pump 'type' field that matches the service; default to centrifugal if unspecified, since it covers the vast majority of process pumps.
Centrifugal is the default for continuous gas compression; use reciprocating for high-pressure-ratio, low-flow duties.

size_variable_in_dwsim: Heat Added / Duty (kW) -> convert to area, see heat_exchanger.notes
notes: Use 'fired_heater' approximate scaling instead (see equipment_without_verified_turton_correlation) if outlet temperature / duty indicates a process furnace rather than a utility exchanger.

size_variable_in_dwsim: Heat Removed / Duty (kW) -> convert to area, see heat_exchanger.notes
Calculated heat transfer area (m2), or UA and LMTD to back into area
notes: Map DWSIM's plate-and-frame HX to 'multiple_pipe' as the closest available correlation if no dedicated plate-HX correlation is added later.

Conceptual-estimate practice is to fold control/block valve cost into the piping & instrumentation allowance (commonly 20-40% of total bare module cost) rather than cost each valve individually. Cost individually only if doing a detailed estimate with vendor quotes.

Piping is conventionally estimated as a percentage of purchased equipment cost (commonly 20-30% for fluid processes) rather than via a K1/K2/K3 correlation, because cost depends on plant layout/routing, not just diameter and length. If you want a length-based estimate anyway, use a $/m-by-nominal-diameter table from a vendor or cost-data handbook.
Instrumentation item; fold into the instrumentation cost allowance (commonly 5-15% of total bare module cost) rather than costing individually.

cost_type: horizontal or vertical (per your L/D orientation choice)
size_variable_in_dwsim: Calculated vessel volume (m3) from flowsheet vapour/liquid flow + chosen residence time and L/D ratio
notes: DWSIM does not size the physical vessel itself — you must size the vessel (diameter, length, volume) from the converged stream flows using a residence-time/settling-velocity method (e.g. Souders-Brown for vapour-liquid), then feed the resulting volume into the vessel correlation.

size_variable_in_dwsim: Calculated vessel volume (m3) — size as above, with boot volume for the second liquid phase added to the main volume

cost_type: vertical (typical for atmospheric storage) or horizontal
size_variable_in_dwsim: Volume (m3) from tank dimensions; set design pressure ~0 barg

cost_type: vertical vessel + sieve or valve trays
size_variable_in_dwsim: Shell: volume from calculated diameter x (Nt x tray spacing + disengagement allowance). Trays: tray area from diameter, Nt = number of stages from the converged column (or Fenske-Underwood-Gilliland N for the shortcut column).
notes: Condenser and reboiler are normally separate DWSIM Cooler/Heater (or dedicated HX) objects in the flowsheet — cost them under 'heat_exchanger', not as part of the column itself, to avoid double counting. A kettle reboiler should use the 'kettle_reboiler' heat-exchanger type.

cost_type: vertical vessel + sieve or valve trays
size_variable_in_dwsim: Same approach as Distillation Column above

dwsim_object: Conversion Reactor / Equilibrium Reactor / Gibbs Reactor
size_variable_in_dwsim: Volume (m3) from assumed/specified residence time x volumetric throughput (DWSIM does not size these reactors mechanically — you must assume a residence time appropriate to the reaction)
notes: Add 'agitator' cost on top if the reactor is mechanically stirred (typical for liquid-phase CSTR-type duty); omit it for a reactor representing a fixed/packed bed or simple holding vessel.

cost_type: vertical vessel + turbine or impeller agitator
size_variable_in_dwsim: Vessel: Volume (m3) from specified residence time. Agitator: shaft power (kW) from an assumed power-per-unit-volume (commonly 0.5-3 kW/m3 for liquid blending/suspension duty — DWSIM does not calculate this directly).

If modelled as a single large vessel: Volume (m3) directly from the reactor specification. If modelled as a jacketed/multitubular reactor needing close temperature control: cost as a 'multiple_pipe' heat exchanger using the total tube-side area instead.
notes: Choose based on what the PFR physically represents in your process, not the DWSIM model type.
This is a flow-combining logic block (a pipe tee in reality), not the agitator/impeller equipment category above. Negligible standalone capital cost.
Flow-splitting logic block; negligible standalone capital cost.

This is an idealised mass-balance shortcut (you specify split fractions directly) rather than a rate-based model of a real machine. It has no inherent cost correlation — decide what real equipment it is standing in for (e.g. a membrane unit, an absorber, a partial condenser) and cost THAT instead.

size_variable_in_dwsim: Solids mass flow (kg/h) or gas/liquid volumetric flow depending on the real equipment represented
notes: Like Component Separator, this is an idealised solids/liquid split. Identify the real solid-liquid (or solid-gas) separation device intended (cyclone, hydrocyclone, centrifuge, filter) and use the corresponding approximate correlation.
